perturbation.original_text: 1.4 “ Permitted Purpose ” means the use of the Confidential In formation to facilitate the Recipient performing the Services;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 “ Permitted Purpose ” means the use of the Confidential Information to generally understand the OSCE's operations, which may or may not include facilitating the Recipient performing the Services;
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition broadens the scope of 'Permitted Purpose.' Originally, it was strictly tied to 'facilitating the Recipient performing the Services.' Now, it also includes 'generally understanding the OSCE's operations,' potentially allowing the Recipient to use Confidential Information for purposes beyond the scope of the agreement to provide services. This creates a contradiction with section 2.1.2, which restricts the Recipient's use of Confidential Information, by allowing use for understanding OSCE operations
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.2 not to use the Confidential Information fo r any purpose whatsoever other than for the Permitted Purpose.
perturbation.justification: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Permitted Purpose' to include 'generally understanding the OSCE's operations,' which extends beyond 'facilitating the Recipient performing the Services.' This directly contradicts section 2.1.2, which restricts the Recipient's use of Confidential Information to only the 'Permitted Purpose.' The expanded definition now allows the use of Confidential Information for understanding OSCE operations, a purpose not originally permitted, creating a clear and direct conflict, therefore 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: 9.6 Signatures and Dates. This Confidentiality Agreement is executed by email and treated as original for all legal purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.6 Signatures and Dates. This Confidentiality Agreement is executed by email but may not be treated as original for all legal purposes, depending on jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that the agreement executed by email is treated as original for all legal purposes. The changed text introduces uncertainty by suggesting that this may not be the case, depending on the jurisdiction. This creates a contradiction with section 7, which requires a 'written amendment' for any changes, now with this text, it creates an ambiguity if the email itself is legally binding.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7 AMENDMENT No modification, amendment or change to this Co nfidentiality Agreement, or waiver of any of its provisions, or any additional contr actual relationship with the Recipient shall be valid unless approved in the form of a written amendment to this Conf identiality Agreement, signed by a fully authorized representative of each Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 9.6 introduces uncertainty about whether email execution is legally original, depending on jurisdiction. This directly contradicts section 7, which mandates a 'written amendment' for any modifications. The original text states the email is treated as original for all legal purposes which adheres to section 7's written amendment requirement, but now the changed text directly contradicts the idea that the email is legally binding in all jurisdictions, thereby conflicting with the written requirement of section 7.

perturbation.original_text: 8 ENTRY INTO FORCE This Confidentiality Agreement shall enter into force on the date of the last signature by or on behalf of both Parties and shall remain in force for a pe riod of five (5) years, notwithstanding any earlier purported termination of this Confidenti ality Agreement for whatever reason.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 ENTRY INTO FORCE This Confidentiality Agreement shall enter into force upon mutual agreement of both Parties, as documented by email confirmation, and shall remain in force for a period of five (5) years, unless terminated earlier by either party with 30 days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the agreement enters into force on the date of the last signature and remains in force for five years, regardless of any earlier purported termination. The modified text introduces ambiguity by saying it enters into force upon 'mutual agreement of both Parties, as documented by email confirmation,' and allows termination with 30 days' notice. This contradicts the 'notwithstanding any earlier purported termination' clause from the original text, and also the definition of the start date of the enforceability, which contradicts with section 9.3 Waiver, that states that no wavier of any provision of th is Confidentiality Agreement shall be enforceable unless in writing and signed by the party against wh om enforcement is sought. Because now if the term is the emails are enough for waiver, then that is contradictory to what section 9.3 is saying.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.3 Waiver. No waiver of any provision of th is Confidentiality Agreement shall be enforceable unless in writing and signed by the party against wh om enforcement is sought. No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right under this Confidentiality Agreement shall operate as a waiver or preclude enforcement of any other right hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces two key contradictions. First, it changes the effective date of the agreement from 'date of last signature' to 'mutual agreement of both Parties, as documented by email confirmation.' This conflicts with section 9.3, which requires waivers to be 'in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement is sought.' Email confirmation does not meet this requirement. Second, the original text stated the agreement remained in force for five years 'notwithstanding any earlier purported termination,' while the changed text allows termination with 30 days' notice. This creates a direct contradiction regarding the term of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 3 INDEMNIFICATION The Recipient agrees to indemnify the OSCE in re spect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims, liabilities the OSCE may suffer or incur as a resu lt of an act or omission by the Recipient or its employees in connection with the Confidential Inform ation and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3 INDEMNIFICATION The Recipient agrees to indemnify the OSCE in re spect of any expenses, losses, damages, costs, claims, liabilities the OSCE may suffer or incur as a resu lt of a deliberate act or gross negligence by the Recipient or its senior employees directly connected to the OSCE project in connection with the Confidential Inform ation and the Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the Recipient indemnifies the OSCE for losses resulting from 'any act or omission' connected to the Confidential Information. The modified text limits this indemnification to 'a deliberate act or gross negligence by the Recipient or its senior employees directly connected to the OSCE project.' This creates a contradiction because under the original agreement, the Recipient would be liable for negligence but under the modified agreement they are no longer liable for simply being negligent. This new definition contradicts the expectations set out in the rest of the document. Because the act now requires a 'deliberate act or gross negligence', and it means that some of the other clauses in the contract may require less protection or less focus of attention.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1.6 to treat Confidential Information as being confidential and proprietary to the OSCE by using the same degree of care, but in any case no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent unauthorised use, dissemination or publication thereof, as it uses to protect its own Confidential Information of a similar nature.
perturbation.justification: The original text broadly indemnifies the OSCE for any act or omission by the Recipient related to Confidential Information. The changed text narrows this to 'a deliberate act or gross negligence by the Recipient or its senior employees directly connected to the OSCE project.' This directly contradicts section 2.1.6, which requires the Recipient to treat Confidential Information with 'no less than a reasonable degree of care' to prevent unauthorized use. By limiting indemnification to deliberate acts or gross negligence, the changed text removes the Recipient's liability for simple negligence, which directly conflicts with the 'reasonable degree of care' standard outlined in section 2.1.6. This creates a strong in-text contradiction as the Recipient is now not liable for a certain threshold of negligence that they are expected to prevent according to section 2.1.6.

perturbation.original_text: 6 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES The Parties shall use their best efforts to settle am icably all disputes arising out of or in connection with the Contract or its interpretation. Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or in relation to the Contract shall be settled through negotiations be tween the Parties. If the Parties fail to settle the dispute amicably within sixty (60) Days of comme ncement of the negotiations, the dispute shall be settled through arbitration. Arb itration shall be performed in accordance with the UNCITRAL arbitration rules. One (1) sole arbitrator shall be appointed who shall have full powers to make final and binding decisions. The appointing authority shall be the Permanent Court of Arbitration at The Hague. The place of arbitration shall be Vienna and the language used in the arbitration proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.changed_text: 6 SETTLEMENT OF DISPUTES The Parties shall use their best efforts to settle am icably all disputes arising out of or in connection with the Contract or its interpretation. Any dispute, controversy or claim arising out of or in relation to the Contract shall be settled through mediation. If the Parties fail to settle the dispute amicably within sixty (60) Days of comme ncement of the negotiations, either party may pursue legal remedies in their jurisdiction of choice, unless both parties agree to arbitration. Arbitration shall be performed in accordance with the UNCITRAL arbitration rules. One (1) sole arbitrator shall be appointed who shall have full powers to make final and binding decisions. The appointing authority shall be the Permanent Court of Arbitration at The Hague. The place of arbitration shall be Vienna and the language used in the arbitration proceedings shall be English.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates arbitration for unresolved disputes after 60 days of negotiation. The modified text replaces 'arbitration' with 'mediation' initially, and then allows either party to pursue legal remedies in their jurisdiction of choice if mediation fails, unless both agree to arbitration. This introduces a contradiction. It creates a legal contradiction because section 9.5 Governing Law states that 'This Confidentiality Agre ement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the substantive laws of the Republic of Austria.', with the new clause it opens the possibility to be other jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9.5 Governing Law. This Confidentiality Agre ement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the substantive laws of the Republic of Austria.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated arbitration. The changed text allows either party to pursue legal remedies in their jurisdiction if mediation fails, unless both agree to arbitration. This contradicts section 9.5, which states that the agreement shall be governed by the laws of the Republic of Austria. The modified text opens the possibility of other jurisdictions governing the agreement, creating a contradiction as defined by 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.
